title: Process String with Special Operations II
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal challenge: the single recommended solution is also the speed extreme and the memory extreme. Per the repo standard (no coincident files), only solution.cpp is shipped; there is no separate solution-runtime or solution-memory file.

variants.languageReason: The problem is a two-pass scan over an ASCII operation string with 64-bit length/index arithmetic and no required dynamic storage. C++ gives native loops, direct LeetCode signature support, cheap string indexing, and no interpreter or GC overhead, making it the strongest practical fit for both raw runtime and low auxiliary memory.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Native byte scans, direct string::size support, 64-bit arithmetic, and a single char return keep both runtime and auxiliary memory minimal for the LeetCode signature.

Considered for raw pointer loops and low runtime baseline, but public LeetCode references for this problem focus on C++/Java/Python/Go style signatures rather than C. Even with C support, the same O(1) auxiliary algorithm would not provide a meaningful memory-axis improvement over C++ here.
Considered for native speed and memory safety, but checked indexing and platform wrapper overhead do not improve this small byte-program scan.
Considered for compiled loops and simple strings, but bounds checks and runtime metadata are less attractive than C++ for top-percentile constants.
Considered because managed runtimes can scan strings quickly, but UTF-16/object overhead and JIT/runtime startup are unnecessary for ASCII input and O(1) state.
Considered for concise implementation, but VM/interpreter overhead in two full scans is weaker for n <= 100000 and a top 1% performance target.

variants.approachJustification: Compute the final virtual length, reject invalid k, then walk s backward. Undo # by halving length and taking k modulo the original half, undo % by mirroring k, undo a letter by checking whether it created the current last slot, and undo * by increasing the previous virtual length along the valid-index path.

reasoningSummary: The final string is a virtual object. Once final length is known, the target index can be traced backward through the operation program: duplication folds k into the first half, reversal mirrors it, a letter answers only when it is the appended last position, and a relevant delete is undone by increasing the previous length. This avoids both final-string construction and an O(n) prefix-length array.
